coredata.dc:title: Microwave synthesis of blue emissive carbon dots from 5-sulpho anthranilic acid and 1,5-diphenyl carbazide for sensing of levocetirizine and niflumic acid
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Vibhuti Atulbhai, Sadhu
Swapna, Bhattu
Kumar Kailasa, Suresh
coredata.dc:description: In this work, water soluble carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by using 5- sulpho anthranilic acid (SAA) and 1,5-diphenylycarbazide (DPC) as precursors via microwave-assisted method and named as “SD-CDs”. We studied the effect of SAA and DPC molar ratio (1:3, 2:2 and 3:1) for the preparation of blue fluorescent CDs, showing the best emission properties at molar 3:1 ratio of SAA and DPC. The as-prepared SD-CDs emit bright blue color under UV light at 365 nm, and exhibit emission peak at 392 nm when excited at 319 nm. The as-synthesized SD-CDs act as a fluorescent sensor for detection of levocetirizine and niflumic acid through the fluorescence “turn-on–off” mechanism. The developed probe exhibited good linearity in the concentrations (levocetirizine − 1.0–100 µM and niflumic acid − 0.5–100 µM) with detection limits of 3.92 nM and 0.19 µM for levocetirizine and niflumic acid, respectively. Importantly, the developed analytical method was successfully used for the detection of levocetirizine in tablets and niflumic acid in biofluids of human (serum, plasma and urine), showing good recoveries from 97 to 99 %. Thus, this SD-CDs-based fluorescence method has the potential for levocetirizine and niflumic acid assays in biological and pharmaceutical samples.

head.ce:title: Microwave synthesis of blue emissive carbon dots from 5-sulpho anthranilic acid and 1,5-diphenyl carbazide for sensing of levocetirizine and niflumic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology, Surat 395 007, Gujarat, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology, Surat 395 007, Gujarat, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, water soluble carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by using 5- sulpho anthranilic acid (SAA) and 1,5-diphenylycarbazide (DPC) as precursors via microwave-assisted method and named as “SD-CDs”. We studied the effect of SAA and DPC molar ratio (1:3, 2:2 and 3:1) for the preparation of blue fluorescent CDs, showing the best emission properties at molar 3:1 ratio of SAA and DPC. The as-prepared SD-CDs emit bright blue color under UV light at 365 nm, and exhibit emission peak at 392 nm when excited at 319 nm. The as-synthesized SD-CDs act as a fluorescent sensor for detection of levocetirizine and niflumic acid through the fluorescence “turn-on–off” mechanism. The developed probe exhibited good linearity in the concentrations (levocetirizine − 1.0–100 µM and niflumic acid − 0.5–100 µM) with detection limits of 3.92 nM and 0.19 µM for levocetirizine and niflumic acid, respectively. Importantly, the developed analytical method was successfully used for the detection of levocetirizine in tablets and niflumic acid in biofluids of human (serum, plasma and urine), showing good recoveries from 97 to 99 %. Thus, this SD-CDs-based fluorescence method has the potential for levocetirizine and niflumic acid assays in biological and pharmaceutical samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Cetirizine is obtained from a racemic mixture of R-cetirizine (levocetirizine) and S-cetirizine (dextrocetirizine). Levocetirizine has high selectivity and affinity towards H1-histamine receptor, exhibiting ∼ 30 times higher affinity as compared with S-cetirizine, which shows no anticholinergic activitywhereas dextrocetirizine is inactive [1]. Levocetirizine dihydrochloride molecular formula is C21H25ClN2O3 and chemical name is 2-[2-[4-[(R)-(4-chlorophenyl)phenylmethyl]-piperazinyl-1-yl]ethoxy]acetic acid dihydrochloride [2]. It is recognized as non-sedative antihistamine drug that was wieldy applied for the treatment of various health issues including chronic idiopathic urticarial, runny nose, hay fever and allergic rhinitis [3,4]. Similarly, niflumic acid (2-{[3-[trifluoromethyl]phenyl] amino}-3- pyridine carboxylic acid) is widely used as a potential active anti-inflammatory drug, exhibiting potent anti-pyretic and analgesic activities [5]. Due to its poor solubility in water and good absorptivity, Biopharmaceutics Classification System (BCS) has placed in a non-steroid class II drug [6]. The working mechanism of niflumic acid is based on reversible inhibition of the cyclooxygenase-2 enzyme, which reduces in human body [7]. In view of this, a series analytical techniques such as UV–visible [8–10], Voltammetry [11], fluorescence [12], conductometry [13], potentiometry [14], gas chromatography (GC) [15,16], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [17-23], reversed-phase HPLC (RP-HPLC) [24,25], flow injection-chemiluminescence (FI-CL) [26], voltammetry [27-29], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometric (LC-MS) [30] and Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopic [31] have been used to quantify both drugs (levocetirizine and niflumic acid in biofluids of humans (urine, plasma, tears and serum) and pharmaceutical samples. Although these analytical tools have been used for the quantification of both drugs in their pharmaceutical formulations, unfortunately some of these techniques require specific levels for either color or fluorescence development and sample preparations. Thus, it is an essential to develop a facile method to detect both drugs.

Among analytical techniques, fluorescence spectrometry is one of the most promising analytical technique for the analysis of numerous chemical species due to its versatility and sensitivity [31]. However, it has a common drawback that requires fluorescent derivatives prior to the analysis of target analytes. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have been explored as promising fluorescent nanostructures for promoting of fluorescence spectrophotometer for assaying of a wide variety target analytes and imaging of various cells since their discovery in 2004 [32-35]. Especially, they showed outstanding properties like low toxicity, water solubility, biocompatibility, photoluminescence, high resistance to photobleaching, and chemical inertness [32]. They have been applied in analytical chemistry and multidisciplinary research as bioimaging probe [36,37], drug delivery [38-42], gene transmission [43], theranostics [44,45], catalysis [46], semiconductors [47,48] and agricultural science [49,50], respectively. Nowadays, health risk is a major problem due to the environmental pollutants such as metal ions [51], pesticides [52], antibiotics [53] and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) [54-57]. These contaminants are present in environment due to industrial gases, waste water, incomplete combustion of any carbon-based matter, like coal, meat, wood, etc. In order to meet green technology, CDs have been used as green materials for various applications and they prepared by various techniques such as hydrothermal method [58], low-temperature solid phase [59], and microwave-assisted [60]. Among them, microwave-assisted method offers several merits including rapidity, formation of compounds with higher yields and minimization of toxic chemicals/solvents [61]. Green chemistry goal is achieved by integrating CDs with fluorescence technique, which avoids the use of tedious sample preparations, and organic labels, thereby reducing the toxic chemicals use in analytical method development [62,63]. These analytical methods demonstrated that the analytical parameters of fluorescence technique are greatly improved for recognition of trace level analytes [63]. To make an analytical approach with sustainability, the CDs are used as a probe for fluorescence detection of both drugs, thereby expanding the analytical applications of fluorescence spectroscopy in pharmaceutical sciences.

In this work, blue-emitting CDs are synthesized by using 5-sulpho anthranilic acid (SAA) and 1,5-diphenyl carbazide (DPC) via microwave method and called as SD-CDs. The as-synthesized SD-CDs act as a probe for the detection of levocitirizine via fluorescence enhancement and of niflumic acid via fluorescence quenching mechanisms (Scheme 1 ). The formation of SD-CDs is confirmed by spectroscopic and high resolution transmission electron microscopic (HR-TEM) techniques. We have promoted SD-CDs as a novel dual sensor for fluorescence assay levocetirizine and niflumic acid. After the addition of levocetirizine, the fluorescence emission of SD-CDs shifts from 392 to 422 nm with enhanced intensity whereas niflumic acid causes a remarkable decrease in the emission spectrum. Under 365 nm of UV light, blue fluorescence enhancement and quenching are noticed by the addition levocetirizine and niflumic acid. The developed SD-CDs sensor exhibited the limits of detection (LOD) are 3.92 nM and 0.19 µM for levocetirizine and niflumic acid, respectively. Notably, SD-CDs-based fluorescence analytical method is successfully used for the detection of both drugs in pharmaceutical and biological samples.

5-Sulfo anthranilic acid was obtained from Himalaya Chemicals Ltd, India. Indapamide, levetiracetam, 5-fluorouracil, 1,5- diphenyl carbazide, Isoniazid, l-valine, metformin hydrochloride levocetirizine, niflumic acid, perindopril and tolbutamide were received as gift samples from Torrent Pharmaceutical Ltd., Ahmedabad, India and Glenmark Pharmaceuticals, Vadodara, India. Dialysis 70-membrane was supplied by Hi Media Laboratories Pvt., India. KBr, NiSO4(H2O)6, CaCl2·2H2O, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, Na2SO4·10H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Mn(NO3)2·4H2O, AlCl3, ethanol and acetone were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Xyzal 5 mg-levocetirizine dihydrochloride tablet was purchased from a local medical store, Surat, Gujarat, India. Human plasma and serum were obtained from Shery laboratory, Surat, Gujarat, India. Ultrapure water utilized for all experiments was obtained from a Milli-Q water purification system.

The fluorescence spectra of SD-CDs were acquired by fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of samples were conducted on a Maya Pro 2000 spectrophotometer (Ocean Optics, USA). The size and morphology of SD-CDs were characterized by HR-TEM (JEM- 2100, JEOL, Japan). Functional groups of SD-CDs were confirmed by FT-IR spectrometer (Alpha II 8400S, Bruker, Germany).

The SD-CDs are synthesized by using 5- sulpho anthranilic acid and 1,5- diphenyl carbazide via microwave method. Briefly, certain amounts of SAA:DPC (1:1 (0.10:0.10 gm), 1:3 (0.05:0.15 gm) and 3:1 (0.15:0.05 gm) were weighed and dissolved in 10 mL (EtOH:H2O, 1:1, v/v) by sonicating for 15 min. Then the solutions were transferred into beakers and applied microwave heating (600 W) for 10 min. The resultant products were cooled naturally to room temperature and then added water. The obtained black residues were centrifuged for 10 min at 6000 rpm and SD-CDs were extracted by using a dialysis against water for 2 h. The obtained SD-CDs were stored in refrigerator for further studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of levocetirizine and niflumic acid by SD-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To promote SD-CDs as a dual fluorescent sensor, 500 µL of various drugs (5-fluorouracil, indapamide, isoniazid, l-valine, levetiracetam, levocetirizine, metformin hydrochloride, niflumic acid, perindropril and tolbutamide, 0.1 mM) were mixed with 1000 µL of SD-CDs and then vortexed for 60 s. The fluorescence emission spectra of the above mixtures were measured by applying λEx at 319 nm. Among the above drugs, levocetirizine was enhanced the intensity with slight shifting of emission spectra whereas niflumic acid was quenched the fluorescence intensity (∼82 %), which signifies that SD-CDs could serve as a dual sensor for both drugs assay. Levocetirizine (1.0 mM) was prepared by dissolving 3.89 mg of levocetirizine in 10 mL water and niflumic acid (1.0 mM) was prepared by dissolving 2.82 mg of niflumic acid in 10 mL water and ethanol. Then, 0.5 mL of different concentrations of levocetirizine (1.0–100 µM) and niflumic acid (0.5–100 µM) were introduced into 2 mL sample vials that contained 1.0 mL of SD-CDs and vortexed for few seconds. The fluorescence emission spectra of SD-CDs-drug systems were investigated under excitation wavelength of 319 nm. These spectral characteristics were utilized for the quantification of both drugs by plotting calibration graphs between emission intensities and concentrations of drugs.

The quantum yield (QY) of SD-CDs was calculated by using quinine sulphate as a reference. The following formula was used to estimate QY of SD-CDs.

Where ‘A’ represents the absorbance, ‘I’ represents the integrated fluorescent intensity, ‘η’ stands for the refractive index and “R” corresponds to the referenced quinine sulphate. Using this equation, the fluorescence QY of SD-CDs was 21.31 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of levocetirizine and niflumic acid recovery in spiked samples
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent SD-CDs were used for the detection of levocetirizine and niflumic acid in real samples such as tablet contain levocetirizine drug, and biofluids (human serum, plasma and urine) for niflumic acid. Plasma, urine and serum samples were obtained from Shery laboratory in Surat, Gujarat, India. Typically, Xyzal 5 mg tablet was prepared in three different concentrations (1.25, 2.5 and 5.0 mg/mL) by dissolving in in H2O. The concentration of levocetirizine in Xyzal tablet from the calibration graph via standard addition method. The collected biofluids (human serum, plasma and urine) samples were diluted and spiked with various concentrations of niflumic acid (0.5, 1.0, 1.5 and 2.0 µM) and analysed by the aforesaid procedure. The analytical data were shown in Tables S1-S2 of Supporting Information.

FT-IR spectra of SAA, DPC and SD-CDs are shown in Fig. S4 of Supporting Information. The FT-IR spectrum of SAA showed peak at 3039 cm−1, which ascribed to the OH stretching. The peaks at 1742 cm−1 show CH bending for aromatic compound and at 1558–1530 cm−1 represent CC stretching for cyclic alkene. Also, the peak appeared at 1419 cm−1 belong to OH bending, which indicates COOH group of SAA. The other peaks appeared at 1344, 1184, 883 cm−1 correspond SO stretching of sulfonic acid, CO stretching and CC bending, respectively. The FT-IR spectrum of DPC exhibits the peak at 3456 cm−1, which assigned to the NH stretching due to amine group and the peak at 3200–3550 cm−1 for OH stretching groups. The absorption peaks at 1658, 1650–1580, 1315, 752 cm−1 shows CN stretching, NH bending, CN stretching due to aromatic amine and CH bending groups on the surface of DPC, respectively. However, FT-IR spectrum of SD-CDs showed different spectral characteristics as compared to FT-IR spectra of SAA and DPC, suggesting the formation of SD-CDs.

The elemental composition of SD-CDs was examined by X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS) (Fig. S5a of Supporting Information. The XPS survey spectrum of SD-CDs displays the peaks at 168.3 eV, 284.79 eV, 400.31 eV, and 531.94 eV correspond to the elements S 2p, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s of SD-CDs, respectively. These results revealed that synthesized SD-CDs exhibit S, C, N, and O elements, showing the elemental percent composition of 2.25 %, 64.5 %, 17.51 % and 15.75 % for S, C, N and O, respectively. XPS of C 1s is confirmed the existence of CC (284.79 eV) and CO (289.01 eV) groups on the surface of SD-CDs (Fig. S5b of Supporting Information). The binding energy peaks at 284.79 eV, 400.31 eV, and 531.94 eV are ascribed due to the presence of some organic functional groups such as CC, CO, NH, CNC, and COH/COC on the surface of SD-CDs (Fig. S5b-d of Supporting Information). The FT-IR and XPS data revealed that the synthesized SD-CDs have active surface functional groups [64]. The size and morphology of SD-CDs are investigated by HR-TEM analysis (Fig. 2). From the HR-TEM micrographs in Fig. 3 a-b, the SD-CDs are monodispersed nanostructures with a mean size of 2.90 ± 1.10 nm. Zeta potential of SD-CDs is −19.0 mV (Fig. S6a of Supporting Information) and the lifetime of SD-CDs is 5.75 ns (Fig. S7 of Supporting Information). These results demonstrated that blue fluorescence monodispersed CDs were fabricated from SAA and DPC as precursors using microwave method.

Fluorescence responses of SD-CDs toward various drug molecules (5-fluorouracil, indapamide, isoniazid, l-valine, levetiracetam, levocetirizine, metformin hydrochloride, niflumic acid, perindropril and tolbutamide) were investigated by fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 3a). Interestingly, none of them could induce any significant fluorescence change, except two drug molecules such as levocitirizine and niflumic acid, showing the SD-CDs have high selectivity toward both drugs with different fluorescence response. As seen in Fig. 3a, the SD-CDs solution exhibited remarkable fluorescence enhancement with red-shift from 392 to 422 nm for levocetirizine and obvious fluorescence quenching of the emission peak at 392 nm for niflumic acid. Inset of Fig. 3b displays fluorescence color of SD-CDs solutions with the addition of the above drug molecules under 365 nm of UV light. Thus, the as-prepared SD-CDs could sense both drugs with different fluorescence characteristics. To further estimate pH effect on the fluorescence enhancing of SD-CDs by levocetirizine and fluorescence quenching by niflumic acid, the emission spectra of SD-CDs in presence of levocetirizine and niflumic acid were studied using phosphate buffered saline (PBS) pH in the range of 2–12 (Fig. S8 of Supporting Information), revealing the fluorescence intensity of SD-CDs does not show any obvious change with PBS pH from 2.0 to 12.0, which signifies that pH did not influence during the sensing of both drugs by SD-CDs as a probe. In view of this, the fluorescence levocetirizine and niflumic acid assays are carried out without buffer pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity study of levocetirizine and niflumic acid
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimum environments, the emission spectra of SD-CDs were measured by addition of various concentrations of levocetirizine (1.0–100 µM) (Fig. 4 a). After addition of levocetirizine, the fluorescence spectra of SD-CDs were slightly red-shifted (392 to 422 nm), accompanied with the fluorescence enhancement (∼50 %). Herein, a fluorescence enhancement probe was developed using SD-CDs. As seen in Fig. S9a of Supporting Information, the fluorescence response of SD-CDs to levocetirizine has a good linearity ΔλEm (392 – 422 nm) and concentration of levecetirizine (1.0–100 µM). The linear regression equation was (ΔλEm (392 – 422 nm)) = 0.4863x + 8.484 (R2 = 0.9914) for levocetirizine. The LOD was 3.92 nM, based on the formula (3σ / S, where, S indicates slope of calibration curve and σ represents standard deviation of SD-CDs). Inset of Fig. 4b demonstrates that blue fluorescence of SD-CDs was gradually increased with increasing amount of levocetirizine.

Encouraged by fluorescence turn-off characteristic of SD-CDs by niflumic acid, the SD-CDs were used as a fluorescence turn-off sensing platform for the detection of niflumic acid. For niflumic acid detection, fluorescence quenching studies have been performed by measuring emission spectra of SD-CDs with increasing concentration of niflumic acid (0.5–100 µM) (Fig. 4c). With the increase of niflumic acid concentration, the fluorescence emission intensity of SD-CDs was gradually decreased, displaying a good linearity between fluorescence quenching and niflumic acid concentrations (0.5–100 µM) with the linear regression equation (I0/I = 0.0101x + 1.0884 (R2 = 0.9884)) for niflumic acid (Figure S9b of Supporting Information), which offers the LOD of 0.19 µM. present in Figure S10b of Supporting Information. The developed method was compared with other analytical techniques [12,16,23,64-72] to detect levocetirizine and niflumic acid (Tables 1 and 2
). These data illustrated that the LODs for both drugs obtained by the present method are far below the reported methods [16,23,65-68,71,72], except LC-MS [70], HPLC [64,69] and voltammetry [12], which reveals that it is a sensitive fluorescent probe for practical applications.

The sensing mechanisms of SD-CDs toward levocitirizine and niflumic acid were investigated by using UV–Visible, fluorescence, FT-IR, zeta potential, lifetime and HR-Tem techniques. The absorption spectra of SD-CDs, SD-CDs + levocetirizine and SD-CDs + niflumic acid showed that λmax of SD-CDs was significantly changed with the introduction of both drugs (Fig. S10a-b of Supporting Information), illustrating strong interactions between active functional groups of both drugs and SD-CDs. Further, FT-IR spectra of SD-CDs, SD-CDs + levocetirizine and SD-CDs + niflumic acid were investigated to confirm the interactions between surface functional groups of SD-CDs with drugs (Fig. S11 of Supporting Information). The FT-IR spectral data illustrated that stretching vibrations of both probe and analytes are significantly changed due to the interaction of SD-CDs with both drugs. Besides, HR-TEM images of SD-CDs were studied with addition of levocetirizine and niflumic acid (Fig. 3c-d), showing the morphology of SD-CDs was completely changed by the addition of both drugs, which confirms the formation of SD-CDs-drug nanoaggregations.

The fluorescence enhancement is due to the formation of SD-CDs-levocetirizine aggregations, which may ascribe to the aggregation induced emission enhancement (AIEE). The AIEE phenomenon demonstrates that the fluorescence enhancement of SD-CDs was ascribed due to the aggregation of SD-CDs induced by levocetirizine. To confirm fluorescence quenching due to Forster resonance electron transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE) mechanisms, the absorption spectra of drugs (levocetirizine and niflumic acid) and fluorescence spectra (excitation and emission) of SD-CDs were studied (Figs. S12 of Supporting Information), suggesting the fluorescence quenching is not due to FRET, but due to IFE. Since IEF mechanism occurs due to a good overlap of the absorption spectrum of analyte with fluorescence (excitation/emission) spectra of fluorescent probe. As seen in Fig. S12 of Supporting Information, the absorption spectrum of niflumic acid (λmax = 338 nm) is almost overlapped with excitation spectrum of SD-CDs (λEx = 319 nm), leading to protect the excitation light for SD-CDs, which results to quench the fluorescence [73–76]. For levocetirizine, the absorption spectrum was not completely overlapped with fluorescence spectra of SD-CDs, which reveals that detection of niflumic acid may due to AIEE. Moreover, the lifetime analysis of SD-CDs was examined in the presence and absence of levocetirizine and niflumic acid (Fig. S7 of Supporting Information). The average fluorescence lifetime of SD-CDs was 5.75 ns; however, it was increased to 7.15 ns in the presence of levocetirizine and slightly decreased to 5.21 ns in the presence of niflumic acid. It was clearly noticed that the average lifetime of SD-CDs was almost unchanged after addition of niflumic acid, revealing the detection is ascribed due to IFE mechanism, which confirms the formation of ground state complex between SD-CDs and niflumic acid [63]. The zeta potential of SD-CDs (−19.0 mV) was decreased to −14.0 and −14.8 mV with the addition of levocetirizine and niflumic acid (Fig. S6b-c of Supporting Information). obtained at −19.0 mV, which shows that the presence of negatively charged amino (NH) and carbonyl (CO) functional groups on their surface (Figure S8 (a-c) of Supporting Information). The zeta potential values after addition of levocetirizine and niflumic acid were observed at −14.0 mV and −14.8 mV, respectively, which shows that the interaction between SD-CDs and both drugs. These analytical data demonstrate that the fluorescence sensing mechanisms are ascribed due to AIEE for levocetirizine and IFE for niflumic acid by using SD-CDs as a probe.

To investigate the selectivity of SD-CDs toward levocetirizine and niflumic acid in the presence of other common interfering agents, various cations (Na+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, K+ and Ca2+), anions (Br-, SO4 2-, Cl- and NO3 –), and drugs (5-fluorouracil, indapamide, levetiracetam, metformin hydrochloride, fluconazole, norfloxacin and flutamide) were selected for either fluorescence enhancement or quenching studies of SD-CDs at λEx 319 nm. As shown in Fig. 5 a-b, no fluorescence enhancement was observed upon the addition of mixture of above interfering agents, however the emission spectra of SD-CDs were red-shifted with fluorescence enhancement only with levocetirizine even in the presence of other interferents. Similarly, metal ions, anions and other drugs did not induce fluorescence quenching of SD-CDs, however with the addition of niflumic acid, the fluorescence intensity was drastically decreased in the presence of other chemical species, illustrating the SD-CDs exhibited good selectivity in the presence of interfering agents. To further investigate selectivity of SD-CDs among levocetirizine and niflumic acid, the selectivity of SD-CDs was examined in the presence of both analytes ((levocetirizine: niflumic acid, 100:100, 100:75, 100:50, 75:100, and 50:100 µM)) (Fig. S13a-b of Supporting Information, suggesting the SD-CDs showed higher selectively towards niflumic acid as compared levocetirizine. However, the fluorescence enhancement/quenching responses of SD-CDs are displayed selectively toward individual target analyte even in the presence of other interfering species, which make them as promising probes for recognition of both drugs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of levocetirizine and niflumic acid in pharmaceutical samples and biofluids
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the practical applications of SD-CDs for assaying levocetirizine in pharmaceutical sample and niflumic acid in biofluids, Xyzal 5 mg (98.0 µM) tablet was prepared different concentrations (1.25, 2.5 and 5 mg/mL) and the concentrations of levocetirizine in the above tablet was estimated by the described procedure earlier. Similarly, the collected biofluids (serum, plasma and urine) of human were treated with four concentrations of niflumic acid (0.5, 1.0, 1.5 and 2.0 µM) and the quantification was carried out by the described procedure earlier. As depicted in Tables S1-S2 of Supporting Information, the analytical data of levocetirizine and niflumic acid were close to the actual values. The recovery rate of levocetirizine assay was in the range of 97.95 – 99.18 % with the relative standard deviation (RSD) values < 1.00 %. For the assay of niflumic acid, the recoveries were noticed in the range of 91.02–99.68 % with the RSD < 1.00 %. These analytical results demonstrated that SD-CDs displayed good analytical performance for levocetirizine and niflumic acid assays in pharmaceutical and biological samples with good accuracy and precision.

In summary, the fluorescent SD-CDs were synthesized using 3:1 ratio of SAA and DPC as a carbon source via microwave-assisted method. The as-synthesized SD-CDs exhibit bright blue emission peak at 392 nm when excited at 319 nm with a good QY of 21.31 %. The SD-CDs acted as a fluorescent probe for the detection of levocetirizine via fluorescence enhancement and of niflumic acid via “fluorescence quenching” mechanisms. Importantly, the SD-CDs displayed a sensitive sensor toward levocetirizine and niflumic acid detection with the LODs at 3.92 nM and 0.19 µM, respectively. Meanwhile, the developed probe exhibits high selectivity towards levocetirizine and niflumic acid over other chemicals species. The SD-CDs-based fluorescent sensor was successfully applied to assay levocetirizine in Xyzal 5 mg tablet and niflumic acid in biofluids, demonstrating the SD-CDs are successfully promoted fluorescence spectroscopy for the analysis of both drugs in reals samples with high sensitivity. Although this method exhibited good sensitivity and selectivity towards both drugs with different fluorescence characteristics, the limitation of the present method is inability to assay each target drug (levocetirizine/niflumic acid) in the presence of other analyte (levocetirizine/niflumic acid). Overall, the SD-CDs could be used as a potential analytical platform to detect levocetirizine and niflumic acid in real samples with greater analytical features (lower LODs, simplicity, admirable selectivity and rapidity).
